query: The emotions and protests of the masses
reference_summary: Egyptian President Hosni Mubarak's announcement that he will serve out remaining term immediately rejected by angry crowds.Man sets himself on fire near Egyptian over advert claiming Egypt revolution as Vodafone’s.Cairo street clashes leave more than 1,000 injured.Egypt clashes after police released on bail.Protests spread in Egypt as discontent with military rallies.Cairo witnesses blame security forces for bloodshed at Coptic march.Protesters return en masse to Egypt's Tahrir Square as the Muslim Brotherhood and other groups stage a sit-in to protest in Tahrir Square, with some of the first chants for Morsi to 'leave.'Clashes erupt across Egypt over President Mohamed Morsi's decree expanding his authority, a move that sharpens lines between Islamists attack a peaceful anti-Morsi sit-in outside the palace, sparking street battles that leave at least 10 dead.Islamists hold massive rally in the city of Alexandria to show solidarity with religious clerics.Hundreds of thousands hold protests in Tahrir Square and nationwide against Morsi on the 2 year anniversary of the start of the revolt against Mubarak, and clashes erupt in many places.Much of the anger is focused at Morsi, who praised the police for their crackdown.A Muslim mob attacks the main cathedral of the Coptic Orthodox Church when Christians hold a funeral and protest there over four Christians killed in sectarian violence outside of Cairo the day before.Millions of Egyptians take to the streets in Cairo and other cities calling for Morsi to step down in a massive display of anger and frustration.Large-scale demonstrations continue, and Egypt's powerful military gives the two sides 48 hours to resolve their disputes, or it will impose its own solution.Demonstrators celebrate in Cairo's Tahrir Square, restoring the scene of revolutionary chaos that ensued after Mubarak's February 2011 ouster.Morsi's opponents and supporters stage huge rallies to push their respective causes.

query: The authorities' attitude towards protests and trials
reference_summary: Mohamed ElBaradei warns of 'Tunisia-style explosion' in Egypt Mohamed ElBaradei says Egyptians 'yearning for change', but draws criticism for refusing to back street-level protests.Mubarak tries to head off protest.Mubarak supporters stage brutal bid to crush Cairo uprising.Egypt's vice-president complains rioting is bad for business.Essam Sharaf, Egypt's new prime minister, rouses Tahrir Square crowds.Egypt promises justice after Copts and Muslims clash in Cairo.Mubarak trial opens.Wheeled into a courtroom cage lying on a bed, Mubarak denies charges of ordering the killing protesters and other abuses of power.Hosni Mubarak denies police were ordered to fire on protesters.The life sentence imposed on toppled President Hosni Mubarak for complicity in the deaths of hundreds of protesters marks an unprecedented milestone in Egypt's path toward democracy.Egypt issues a decree allowing military police to detain civilians, a move critics say is a barely disguised bid to reinstate decades-old emergency law that expired on May 31.A move by Egypt's ruling generals to revive martial law is blunted as a court strikes down a government decree that had allowed soldiers and military intelligence services to arrest civilians during the nation's political turmoil.Egyptian President Mohamed Morsi issues a blanket pardon for hundreds of activists arrested during the revolution and its turbulent aftermath, in what was widely viewed as a morally wise but politically timed move from a leader attempting to calm his critics amid social and economic turmoil.Much of the anger is focused at Morsi, who praised the police for their crackdown.Morsi condemns the attack, but critics blame virulent anti-Shiite rhetoric by his hard-line Islamist allies, fueled by Syria's civil war.The military's statement appears to back the protesters, telling the government to respond to their demands.

Egypt hit by new wave of protests as military postpones election.Military regime announces first elections since Hosni Mubarak was ousted, but opposition groups are unhappy with rules.Tantawi promises that a civilian president will be elected in June 2012, six months sooner than planned.First voting in parliamentary election.The election concludes in January with nearly half the seats won by the previously banned Muslim Brotherhood.Egypt's volatile presidential race is jolted when the election commission disqualifies three controversial front-runners - the nation's former spy chief and two impassioned Islamists - just five weeks before voters go to the polls.Ten candidates disqualified from the presidential race, including Omar Suleiman and Muslim Brotherhood's Khairat al-Shater.Brotherhood fields reserve candidate Mohamed Morsy.The presidential campaign officially begins.Egyptians begin two days of voting in a landmark contest between a stirring political Islam and a secularist vision embodied by men connected to the old regime.Morsy and Shafik declared the runoff candidates.The battle between Egypt's military leaders and the ascendant Muslim Brotherhood over the country's political fate dramatically sharpens when the nation's constitutional court dissolves the Islamist-dominated parliament while upholding the right of an ally of deposed leader Hosni Mubarak to remain on the presidential election ballot.The Muslim Brotherhood claims victory in Egypt's landmark presidential runoff election, but its historic rise to power is blunted by a decree from the ruling military council to greatly limit the authority of the nation's next leader.Protesters return en masse to Egypt's Tahrir Square as the Muslim Brotherhood and other groups stage a sit-in to protest actions by Egypt's military council ahead of presidential runoff results.Muslim Brotherhood candidate Mohamed Morsi is declared Egypt's first freely elected president, defeating Ahmed Shafik, the last prime minister to serve deposed leader Hosni Mubarak.Military officials disclose main details of their plan if no agreement is reached: replacing Morsi with an interim administration, canceling the Islamist-based constitution and calling elections in a year.Secular and liberal factions wrangle with the sole Islamist group that backed Morsi's ouster over installing a new prime minister, reflecting the difficulties in building a broad coalition behind a new leadership.

query: The suspension, formulation, voting and controversy of the constitution
reference_summary: Two days later, the body of top generals, the Supreme Council of the Armed Forces, dissolves parliament and suspends the constitution, meeting two key demands of protesters.Egypt's military rejects swift transfer of power and suspends constitution.In the first post-Mubarak vote, Egyptians cast ballots on constitutional amendments sponsored by the military setting the framework for the transition to democracy, including scheduling the first parliamentary and presidential elections.Islamists back the amendments, eager to hold elections they are poised to dominate.Many revolutionaries push for a 'no' vote, arguing a constitution should be written first.The measures are overwhelmingly approved.Parliament approves an assembly to draft a new constitution - the first attempt was criticized for including too many Islamists.The Supreme Constitutional Court orders the dissolving of the Islamist-dominated lower house of parliament on grounds that a third of its members were elected illegally.In a marathon all-night session, Islamists in the constituent assembly rush to complete the draft of the constitution, with almost all non-Islamists boycotting the gathering.Morsi sets the referendum date for the disputed charter for Dec. 15.Islamists protest outside the Supreme Constitutional Court, blocking judges from entering to rule on the legality of the constituent assembly.More than 100,000 protesters march on the presidential palace, demanding the cancellation of the referendum and the writing of a new constitution.But the move fails to calm unrest as the president refuses to cancel a referendum later in the month on a proposed constitution drafted by an Islamist-dominated assembly.Unofficial results show that 56% voted yes for the draft constitution.In the final vote on the constitution, it passes with a 64% yes vote.Military officials disclose main details of their plan if no agreement is reached: replacing Morsi with an interim administration, canceling the Islamist-based constitution and calling elections in a year.Egypt's military, which two days earlier had issued an ultimatum that Morsi end the disruptive turmoil paralyzing the country within 48 hours, announces that it has removed him from office and suspended the constitution.

Egyptian officials' assets to be traced by Serious Organised Crime Agency.Thousands join 'day of rage' across the Middle East.Egypt strikes $3bn IMF deal to 'relaunch' economy.Egypt says it has asked the IMF for $3.2 billion to help to fill a budget gap widened by almost a year of political and economic turmoil.U.S. Secretary of State Hillary Rodham Clinton meets for the first time with new President Mohamed Morsi.The U.S. government releases $250 million in aid to Morsi's government in exchange for pledges of political and economic reforms.Secretary of State John F. Kerry serves notice that the Obama administration will keep close watch on how Morsi honors his commitments and that future aid will be dependent on fulfilling promises of building democracy.Egypt is also struggling to meet conditions for a $4.8-billion loan package from the International Monetary Fund.Officials say the changes aim to finalize long-stalled negotiations with the International Monetary Fund for crucial $4.8 billion loan, which requires sensitive reductions to fuel and food subsidies that take up a large chunk of the budget.A deal on the loan has still not been reached.Nineteen Americans are convicted on charges of operating illegally funded organizations for their work helping Egyptians build civil society institutions.The U.S. defendants, all but one already out of the country, are sentenced to five-year prison terms.The move angers Washington and Egypt's secular opposition, as it is seen as an effort to repress rival political forces challenging Morsi's Muslim Brotherhood allies.

Egypt's new cabinet announces 15% rise in salaries and pensions. Tantawi swears in new cabinet but Muslim Brotherhood and others want it purged of Mubarak's ministers.Prime Minister Shafik resigns and military asks former Transport Minister Essam Sharaf to form cabinet.Egypt's prime minister reshuffles cabinet in response to protests.Morsi reshuffles the Cabinet, bringing in more ministers from the Muslim Brotherhood to total 11 members of the 36-member government, angering opposition leaders who had demanded more secular ministers to balance Islamist influence in the government's upper ranks.

query: Protests against Mubarak, resignation and trial
reference_summary: Egyptian President Hosni Mubarak's announcement that he will serve out remaining term immediately rejected by angry crowds.Egyptians hold nationwide demonstrations against the authoritarian rule of Hosni Mubarak, who has led the country for nearly three decades.Tourism loses at least $1bn as one million visitors flee anti-Mubarak protests.Cairo's biggest protest yet demands Mubarak's immediate departure.Mubarak says national dialogue under way, transfers powers to Vice President Suleiman.Hosni Mubarak resigns and Egypt celebrates a new dawn.A military council led by Field Marshal Hussein Tantawi takes over.Thousands protest in Tahrir Square against delays in putting Mubarak on trial.Hosni Mubarak detained over corruption allegations.Hosni Mubarak's name to be removed from all public buildings.Tahrir Square fills again as Egypt holds Mubarak's wife for crimes against state.Hosni Mubarak charged with murder.Mubarak in the dock: historic trial of former Egyptian president begins.Wheeled into a courtroom cage lying on a bed, Mubarak denies charges of ordering the killing protesters and other abuses of power.Hosni Mubarak's puppet trade union federation dismantled.Mubarak on trial.The life sentence imposed on toppled President Hosni Mubarak for complicity in the deaths of hundreds of protesters marks an unprecedented milestone in Egypt's path toward democracy.

July 8: Morsi issues a surprise decree overruling the court's dissolution of parliament and challenging the generals.Lawmakers defy a court order and reconvene the dissolved parliament, a symbolic victory for President Mohamed Morsi, who had ordered it to meet despite a recent court ruling that disbanded the chamber because of electoral violations.In a bold step, Morsi orders the retirement of the top Mubarak-era leadership of the military and cancels the military's last constitutional decree, taking back the powers that the generals gave themselves.The move was seen as way to curb the military's role in political affairs, but it also gave Morsi the power to legislate in the absence of parliament.Egyptian President Mohamed Morsi issues a blanket pardon for hundreds of activists arrested during the revolution and its turbulent aftermath, in what was widely viewed as a morally wise but politically timed move from a leader attempting to calm his critics amid social and economic turmoil.Morsi unilaterally decrees greater powers for himself, giving his decisions immunity from judicial review and barring the courts from dissolving the constituent assembly and the upper house of parliament.Morsi sets the referendum date for the disputed charter for Dec. 15.Days later, Morsi rescinds his initial decrees, but maintains the date of the referendum.Morsi rescinds his 2-week-old decree seizing broad powers and putting his office beyond judicial oversight.Morsi scores a victory in his push for an Islamist state when a controversial new constitution is approved by 64% of voters in a two-round referendum.Morsi invokes emergency powers in Ismailia, Port Said and Suez to quell riots that have killed nearly 50 and raised questions on whether his Islamist-backed government can secure order amid intensifying political turmoil.Morsi schedules elections for a new lower house of parliament to begin in April.Morsi reshuffles the Cabinet, bringing in more ministers from the Muslim Brotherhood to total 11 members of the 36-member government.Morsi condemns the attack, but critics blame virulent anti-Shiite rhetoric by his hard-line Islamist allies, fueled by Syria's civil war.A week earlier, Morsi shared a stage with hard-line clerics at a rally, sitting silently as they denounced Shiites as 'filthy.'Morsi delivers a late-night speech in which he pledges to defend his legitimacy and vows not to step down.Deadline for Morsi and opponents to come to agreement passes, Morsi standing firm.

The move sparks days of protests, with clashes between Morsi's supporters and opponents.At one point, some 200,000 people rally in Tahrir Square, with some of the first chants for Morsi to 'leave.'Clashes erupt across Egypt over President Mohamed Morsi's decree expanding his authority, a move that sharpens lines between Islamists and those who fear the president is stealing power in order to edge the country closer to Islamic law.Morsi sets a Dec. 15 date for a referendum, fueling further protests.Egypt's Islamist-dominated constitutional assembly passes a rushed draft of a constitution to ease public anger against President Mohamed Morsi's expanded powers and preempt an expected court decision to disband the chamber the following weekend.The next day, Islamists attack a peaceful anti-Morsi sit-in outside the palace, sparking street battles that leave at least 10 dead.Hundreds of thousands hold protests in Tahrir Square and nationwide against Morsi on the 2 year anniversary of the start of the revolt against Mubarak, and clashes erupt in many places.Much of the anger is focused at Morsi, who praised the police for their crackdown.A Muslim mob attacks the main cathedral of the Coptic Orthodox Church when Christians hold a funeral and protest there over four Christians killed in sectarian violence outside of Cairo the day before.One person is killed in clashes at the cathedral, and Pope Tawadros II publicly blames Morsi for failing to protect the building, thought the president condemns the attack.Morsi reshuffles his Cabinet, strengthening the Muslim Brotherhood's hold on power and angering opposition leaders who had demanded more secular ministers to balance Islamist influence in the government's upper ranks.Millions of Egyptians take to the streets in Cairo and other cities calling for Morsi to step down in a massive display of anger and frustration.Organizers vow to keep up the protests until Morsi resigns.

query: Casualties caused by protests and attacks
reference_summary: At least 21 dead and more than 70 injured after bomb explodes outside Coptic church in Alexandria.Man sets himself on fire near Egyptian parliament.A large security force moves into Cairo's Tahrir Square, beating and arresting protesters and using rubber bullets and tear gas.Three protesters are killed in similar protests outside Cairo, among the first in an eventual death toll of about 900 during the uprising.Cairo street clashes leave more than 1,000 injured.Egypt erupts into new violence as dozens injured during Cairo protest.Israelis killed in attacks near Egypt border.Troops crush a protest by Christians in Cairo over a church attack, killing more than 25 protesters.The rally is one of many during the military's nearly 17-month rule, with protesters often calling for the removal of the generals and clashes frequently erupting that killed dozens.Cairo witnesses blame security forces for bloodshed at Coptic march.Thousands demanding an end to military rule pack Tahrir Square in the biggest turnout of a week of protests in which 42 people were killed.The next day, Islamists attack a peaceful anti-Morsi sit-in outside the palace, sparking street battles that leave at least 10 dead.Police crack down hard in Port Said, killing more than 40 protesters, and the city and others nearby go into near revolt.Morsi invokes emergency powers in Ismailia, Port Said and Suez to quell riots that have killed nearly 50 and raised questions on whether his Islamist-backed government can secure order amid intensifying political turmoil.A Muslim mob attacks the main cathedral of the Coptic Orthodox Church when Christians hold a funeral and protest there over four Christians killed in sectarian violence outside of Cairo the day before.One person is killed in clashes at the cathedral, and Pope Tawadros II publicly blames Morsi for failing to protect the building, thought the president condemns the attack.A mob beats to death four Egyptian Shiites in their home in a village on the edge of Cairo.The demonstrations are largely peaceful, although 16 people, half of them in clashes outside the Muslim Brotherhood's Cairo headquarters, are killed in protest-related violence nationwide.Adly dissolves the Islamist-dominated upper house of parliament as soldiers open fire on mostly Islamist protesters in Cairo demanding restoration of Morsi, killing at least one person.
